comms: Arguably, the NP in the genitive is a clause-level dependent.
comms_internal: CV. MRP: Additional evidence for the GEN2 interpretation: Aslanɨ e fikʰir kʰirqomä. SS. “Do you think that “lejla-jɨ” is a regular adnominal modifier or rather it can be viewed as a clause-level dependent (in terms of word order and other syntactic criteria)?” MRP. “I’ve just looked at the corpus again and found three counter examples (copy-pasted in the local alphabet). It seems that GEN2 competes with other cases: LOC in: sa haqq-ır Muradu ixer fikir kiri yuk̂oat̂t̂işə “Murad didn’t want to think very much about that” with sa haqq-ır to be glossed as “dp.prox.obl.nh regard-loc”. DAT in: Pil ĉəşirsu fikir kəkui “Don’t think about putting an eye on it” with the verbal noun ĉəşi-r-i-s-u to be glossed as “put.ipfv-ipfv-ptcp-dp.prox.obl.nh-dat”. I thought it might have to do with human (GEN2) vs. non-human (something spatial), but GEN2 also occurs with pronouns referring to abstract contexts, as in: həki hine fikir kiretmə ki, ya kinə? “Now I’m thinking about it (i.e. the whole aforementioned situation), (thinking) what can I do?” with hin-e to be glossed as dp.dist.obl-gen2. Notably, neither verbal nouns, nor pronouns, nor human nouns can take the LOC case, so DAT and GEN2 might be replacements. This hints at Leyla-yi being a clause-level dependent rather than a regular adnominal modifier”. MD. I am not sure all these examples are equally relevant (thus I think the use of Dat is a purpose close) - but I agree that we may accept the genitive being a clause level dependent here, because we cannot really check it now.

comms_internal: muxu- „know“ is a peculiar verb (same in Kryz, by the way): it is used as a petrified perfective participle + obligatory light verb pfv: qʼi, ipfv: kui in most tenses, but sometimes occurs without the light verb. Here, since it is initiotransformative, the perfective of “get to know” is used to express the present “know”.

comms_internal: MRP: I don’t think [the gentive NP] is separable by adverbs (*Aslan’s right now car exists), but this needs to be verified with consultants. MD. In the absence of additional evidence, I would follow the standard solution for predicative possession in Daghestan - that this is a floating genitive. MRP: Regarding questions, there is evidence from the corpus: Qarıyınzi ya t̂üiji? „What do the old women have?” (with women in -GEN2 and ya “what” between the possessor and the verb). I expect the Genitive to vary according to the relation to the possessee (SUP/GEN1 for body parts etc., GEN2 for owned items), but this distinction cannot be made at “old women” or any other instances in the corpus for phonologic reasons. (Monika herself is not convinced that this is an argument in favor of any solution.
Calqued from colloquial Azeri: “Aslan açarları üçün gəzir.” Lit. “Aslan is walking around for his keys.”

The word order marks Aslan as topic in the Khinalug sentence, but the dog as topic in the Azeri sentence. The Khinalug translation calqued from colloquial Azeri: “Aslanı it tutub.” Lit. “The dog grasped (snatched) Aslan.”, which is used in the sense of “attack by biting”. The other Khinalug term kʼakʼa kʰiri lit. “do a bite” is used when biting into food, or biting off in order to chew and cannot be used in this sentence.

CV. Additional evidence shows that this is indeed a dative form, cf: “pʰsi as ħuǯum kʰušämä.” [bear-ERG I.DAT attack 4.do.PFV-PTCP=PST=DECL].

In Khinalug, the case marker -i functions as instrumental marking. In nouns, the marker is homophonous between the Ergative and the Super-Essive~Genitive1. In other cases, we distinguish between the two categories by substituting the noun with a personal pronoun, where the two cases are distinct. However, instrumental uses, including the context in (54), are incompatible with personal pronominal reference, so this diagnostic is not applicable. One interpretation would be to gloss the marker as ergative based on our knowledge that it is often the ergative and never the super-essive or the genitive that is used for instruments in East Caucasian. On the other hand, instrumental marking in -i are in what is apparently free variation with instrumental marking in -illi, which is morphologically the Super-Elative. As there are other cases of free variation between case markers containing and lacking -illi when used in non-spatial functions, this may be considered as an indication that we deal with the Super in instrumental constructions. We have no conclusive evidence to choose between the two analyses. The glossing of this example follows the second interpretation (even though the use of -illi here in stead of -i is possible in this context).

comms_internal: Monika was not sure about “-i” at “šähär”, but Misha believes this is Gen2: “I just checked with one of our consultant, with a noun that distinguishes them, he provided the -e ending (c’oe).” MRP: From a synchronic perspective, it would make sense to believe that “gäχräma” is a postposition. Etymologically, I suspect that gäχräma is a noun. The word gäχräma is derived from a stem gäʁ- (var. gäx-) “to turn around, rotate”. MD: “Daghestanian languages often have an ambiguous adverb-postposition class, where the would-be postpositions may be used without dependents, and I suspect Khinalug is not an exception”. Alternative is “Divardırı şəhər əhatə kuqomə.” = “divardɨrɨ šähär ähatʰä kʰuqomä” = “divar-dɨr-ɨ šähär ähatʰä kʰu-i-qo=mä” [wall-pl-erg town encirclement 4.do.pfv-ptcp-below-cop=decl] with the TR pattern. In this translation, the consultant copied the Azeri sentence, but probably, nobody would ever say that. A natural sentence would be: “Şəhəri gəxrəma divar qomə.” i.e. “There is a wall / are walls all around the town.” We suggest to exclude this sentence from the results as an non-precise translation.

comms_internal: This is an intermediate solution following a lengthy discussion in the original file (MRP: relativization is an argument in favour of the NP-internal interpretation of the genitive: one cannot say “Father is the only person he obeys” only “Father’s words are the only words he hears”). Calqued from Azeri (look at sb’s word = obey); the more frequent word for “word” is ǯuʁab (cf. Budugh ǯuʁab “answer”). In idioms, a probably more ancient lexeme is maintained, but used only with case forms. Therefore, we do not know if the stem is iš and the subsequent vowel is just a support vowel before a consonantic case suffix, or if the stem is iši.

comms_internal: CV. There are two synonymous variants: “Aslanı quşu gulla fatkuşəmə / bijuvşəmə.” (both verbs mean “throw”). Both variants do not provide information about whether Aslan hit or missed the bird. If he explicitly hit the bird, the speaker could use the verb yeṫ- (1/4/NHPL), zaṫ- (2), vaṫ- (3/HPL) “hit, beat, shoot dead”, i.e. quš vatʼšämä (with the bird as object), or qušu gulla it̂irkuşəmə, (with bird in dativ, and bullet as direct object).
